subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 10! In this unit you become a data detective. Data is just a special word for information that we gather to answer a question or to help us make a good decision. Every day, all around you, people collect data. When Amina counts how many goats come home to the pen each evening, when a shopkeeper in the Hargeisa market keeps track of which fruit sells the fastest, and when a football coach writes down how many goals each player scores, they are all collecting and recording data.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 4 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, invent fresh questions, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why do these skills matter so much? Because data helps us understand the world and make fair, sensible choices. Scientists collect data when they do experiments. Nurses at a clinic in Mogadishu record how many children come for check-ups. Business people collect data about what customers like so they do not waste money making things nobody wants. When Sofia wants to bake cakes for the school fair, she does not guess which flavour to make. She *collects data* by asking people what they like, and then she decides. Good data leads to good decisions.

As you work, keep a pencil, a ruler, and some squared paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading. You must do it. Draw the tally marks, build the tables, plot the dots, and say your conclusions out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure. It is simply a signal that shows you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin our data adventure!
Tell the difference between a statistical question and a question with only one answer.
Plan an investigation: choose a question, decide what data to collect, and plan how to record it.
Collect and record data systematically so it is accurate and easy to read.
Create and read a tally chart, using tally marks correctly in groups of five.
Turn tally marks into a frequency table and use it to answer questions.
Create and interpret a dot plot above a labelled number line.
Draw conclusions from a data display, such as the most common value or the range.

concepts.title: What Is Data and What Is a Statistical Question?
concepts.explanation: Data is information we collect to help us answer a question or make a decision. Data comes in different shapes. Sometimes it is numbers, like how many children are in a class. Sometimes it is categories, like favourite colours or types of pets. Sometimes it is measurements, like the heights of plants in a garden or the temperature each day. Whatever form it takes, data is only useful when we collect it carefully and record it clearly. Not every question is worth collecting data for. A statistical question is a…
concepts.example: Compare these two questions. 'How many books do the children in our class read each month?' is a statistical question, because different children will read different numbers of books, so we must collect data to answer…

concepts.title: Planning an Investigation
concepts.explanation: Once you have a good statistical question, you cannot just rush out and start counting. You need a plan. Planning an investigation means thinking ahead so that the data you collect is accurate, fair, and useful. A good plan follows these clear steps: Choose your question. Make sure it is clear and that it can truly be answered with data.
concepts.example: Decide what data to collect. Think about exactly what information will help answer your question.

concepts.title: Tally Charts
concepts.explanation: A tally chart is one of the best tools for collecting data while it is happening. A tally chart records data by making small marks, called tally marks, to count items in different categories. Tally marks are simply straight vertical lines. The clever part is how we group them: after every four marks, the fifth mark is drawn as a line straight across the previous four. This makes the marks into neat bundles of five, which are very fast to count. For example, to record the number 7, you draw a group of five (four…
concepts.example: Here is how to create a tally chart, step by step:

concepts.title: Frequency Tables
concepts.explanation: A frequency table is very close cousin to a tally chart. The word frequency simply means *how many times* something happens or appears. When you count up the tally marks in each category and write down the number, you have made a frequency table. In fact, most tally charts have a frequency column right beside the tallies, so you can see both at once, just like the shoe-size chart above. Why bother turning tallies into numbers? Because numbers are quicker to compare and easier to calculate with. Once the shoe-size…
concepts.example: Here is a small worked idea. Suppose a tally chart of favourite fruits shows Apple IIII III, Banana IIII I, Orange IIII, Strawberry IIII II, and Grapes IIII. Reading each group of five plus its leftovers gives the…

concepts.title: Dot Plots
concepts.explanation: A dot plot is another clear way to display data, and it is especially good for numbers. In a dot plot, we draw a horizontal number line, and above each number we place one dot for every time that number appears in the data. If a value appears many times, the dots stack up on top of one another, making a little tower. The taller the tower, the more often that value happens. Here is how to create a dot plot, step by step:
concepts.example: Draw a horizontal number line that is long enough to show every value in your data.

concepts.title: Reading Information from a Dot Plot
concepts.explanation: Making a dot plot is only half the skill. The other half is reading one that somebody else has drawn. To read a dot plot, first look at the label on the number line so you understand what the numbers mean. Then count the dots above each number to find how many times that value appears. Then look for patterns, such as which value has the tallest tower. Finally, compare different values to see how the data is spread. Here is a dot plot showing how many hours of sport each learner does each week. Counting the towers…
concepts.example: From this plot we can answer many questions. 1 learner does 0 hours of sport. 3 learners do exactly 2 hours. No learner does 5 or 7 hours. And here is a harder one: how many learners do more than 2 hours of sport each…

methods.title: How to plan an investigation
methods.example: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
steps: Choose a clear statistical question.
Decide what data to collect and who to ask.
Plan how to collect it: count, measure, or ask.
Choose how to record it: table, tally chart, or dot plot.
Collect carefully, then read the data to answer your question.

methods.title: How to make a tally chart and frequency table
methods.example: Write the number 7 correctly using tally marks.
steps: List the categories in a table.
Add one tally mark per item, grouping in fives.
Count the tally marks in each row.
Write that count as the frequency number.

methods.title: How to read a dot plot
methods.example: A pet tally chart shows: Dog IIII II (7), Cat IIII (5), Fish III (3), Rabbit II (2), Hamster IIII (4), Bird I (1). How many children were surveyed in total, and how many more have dogs than rabbits?
steps: Read the label on the number line.
Count the dots in each tower.
Find the tallest tower for the most common value.
For 'more than', add all the towers to the right of the value.

methods.title: Build a tally chart
methods.example: Make a tally-and-frequency chart for this data about favourite fruit: Apple 8, Banana 6, Orange 4, Strawberry 7, Grapes 5.
steps: Solution: Turn each number into groups of five plus leftovers.
Apple 8 = IIII III; Banana 6 = IIII I; Orange 4 = IIII; Strawberry 7 = IIII II; Grapes 5 = IIII.
The frequencies stay 8, 6, 4, 7, 5, and the total is 8 + 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 = 30 children.

methods.title: Make a dot plot
methods.example: Show this data on a dot plot: number of books read = 2, 3, 0, 1, 4, 2, 3, 5, 2, 1, 3, 4, 0, 2, 3, 1, 2, 5, 3, 2.
steps: Solution: First count each value.
0 appears 2 times, 1 appears 3 times, 2 appears 6 times, 3 appears 5 times, 4 appears 2 times, 5 appears 2 times.
Draw a number line labelled 'Number of books read' from 0 to 5, then stack that many dots above each number.
The tallest tower is above 2, so 2 books is the most common.

methods.title: Read a dot plot (siblings)
methods.example: A dot plot of siblings has these towers: 1 sibling has 2 dots, 2 siblings have 4 dots, 3 siblings have 3 dots, 4 siblings have 2 dots, 6 siblings has 1 dot. How many children were surveyed, and what is the range?
steps: Solution: Add every dot: 2 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 12 children.
The range is from the lowest value to the highest value, which is from 1 sibling to 6 siblings.
Notice there are no dots above 5, so nobody had exactly 5 siblings, but 6 is still the highest value present.

workedExamples.title: Is it a statistical question?
workedExamples.prompt: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Is it a statistical question? Decide whether each is a statistical question: (a) How many siblings do the children in our class have? (b) What is the capital of Uganda? Solution: (a) is a statistical question, because different children have different numbers of siblings, so we must collect data. (b) is not a statistical question, because there is only one correct answer (Kampala), which we do not need to survey. The test is always: do we expect different answers, and must we collect data?

workedExamples.title: Show 7 in tally marks
workedExamples.prompt: Write the number 7 correctly using tally marks.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Group in fives. First draw a bundle of five (four lines with the fifth across them), then add two more single lines. So 7 is written IIII II (a five-group plus two). It is *not* correct to write seven separate single lines, because grouping in fives is what makes tally marks fast to count.

workedExamples.title: Read a tally chart
workedExamples.prompt: A pet tally chart shows: Dog IIII II (7), Cat IIII (5), Fish III (3), Rabbit II (2), Hamster IIII (4), Bird I (1). How many children were surveyed in total, and how many more have dogs than rabbits?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add all the frequencies: 7 + 5 + 3 + 2 + 4 + 1 = 22 children in total. For the second part, subtract: dogs minus rabbits is 7 − 2 = 5 more children have dogs than rabbits. The frequency numbers made both answers quick.

workedExamples.title: Build a tally chart
workedExamples.prompt: Make a tally-and-frequency chart for this data about favourite fruit: Apple 8, Banana 6, Orange 4, Strawberry 7, Grapes 5.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Turn each number into groups of five plus leftovers. Apple 8 = IIII III; Banana 6 = IIII I; Orange 4 = IIII; Strawberry 7 = IIII II; Grapes 5 = IIII. The frequencies stay 8, 6, 4, 7, 5, and the total is 8 + 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 = 30 children.

workedExamples.title: Make a dot plot
workedExamples.prompt: Show this data on a dot plot: number of books read = 2, 3, 0, 1, 4, 2, 3, 5, 2, 1, 3, 4, 0, 2, 3, 1, 2, 5, 3, 2.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First count each value. 0 appears 2 times, 1 appears 3 times, 2 appears 6 times, 3 appears 5 times, 4 appears 2 times, 5 appears 2 times. Draw a number line labelled 'Number of books read' from 0 to 5, then stack that many dots above each number. The tallest tower is above 2, so 2 books is the most common. Always count the values before you draw.

workedExamples.title: Read a dot plot (siblings)
workedExamples.prompt: A dot plot of siblings has these towers: 1 sibling has 2 dots, 2 siblings have 4 dots, 3 siblings have 3 dots, 4 siblings have 2 dots, 6 siblings has 1 dot. How many children were surveyed, and what is the range?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add every dot: 2 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 12 children. The range is from the lowest value to the highest value, which is from 1 sibling to 6 siblings. Notice there are no dots above 5, so nobody had exactly 5 siblings, but 6 is still the highest value present.

workedExamples.title: Choose the right method
workedExamples.prompt: Would you use a tally chart, a dot plot, or both for: (a) the eye colours of your class, (b) the number of goals scored in football matches?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) Eye colours are categories, not numbers, so a tally chart is best. (b) Goals scored are numbers, so you could use a tally chart to collect them and a dot plot to show the spread, so both are appropriate. Always ask first: is my data categories or numbers?

workedExamples.title: Compare two data sets
workedExamples.prompt: Class A read (using value × frequency): 0×2, 1×3, 2×4, 3×3, 4×2, 5×1 books. Class B read: 0×1, 1×2, 2×3, 3×5, 4×3, 5×1. Which class read more in total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Class A total = 0 + 3 + 8 + 9 + 8 + 5 = 33 books. Class B total = 0 + 2 + 6 + 15 + 12 + 5 = 40 books. Since 40 is greater than 33, Class B read more books in total. Multiplying each value by its frequency, then adding, is the fair way to compare.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: How many more children chose water than tea?
workedExamples.solution: Frequencies: 0 appears 2, 1 appears 4, 2 appears 3, 3 appears 1; most common is 1 goal.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: Turn this data into a frequency table for number of goals: 1, 0, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1, 0, 2, 1. Which number of goals is most common?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: A dot plot of test scores has towers: 7 has 5 dots, 8 has 4 dots, 9 has 3 dots, 10 has 3 dots. How many children took the test?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: For that same score plot, how many children scored more than 8?
workedExamples.solution: Use the section 2: core practice guidance and explain each step.

activities.title: Family Favourites Survey
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and your family or neighbours.
steps: Choose a statistical question, such as 'What is your favourite fruit?' (mango, banana, papaya, dates, orange).
Draw a tally chart with a row for each fruit.
Ask at least ten people and add one tally mark for each answer, grouping in fives.
Count the tallies to make a frequency table, then write one sentence about the most popular fruit.
Keep your chart. You have just carried out a real investigation from question to conclusion!

activities.title: The One-Minute Challenge
activities.materials: a stopwatch or clock, paper, a pencil, and a few friends or family members.
steps: Pick a fair challenge, such as 'How many star jumps can we do in one minute?'
Time each person for exactly one minute while you count carefully.
Record each name and number in a table, just like Aisha did in the lesson.
Find the greatest, the least, and the total. Who did the most? What was the total?

activities.title: Tally the Traffic (Investigation)
activities.materials: a safe place to sit and watch a road, paper, and a pencil.
steps: Draw a tally chart with rows for cars, buses, bicycles, and people walking.
For ten minutes, add a tally mark each time one goes past, grouping in fives.
Turn your tallies into a frequency table. Which was the most common? Which was the least?
Do it again at a different time of day and compare. Was there more traffic near a prayer time?

activities.title: Dot Plot of the Week
steps: Each day for a week, count something that changes, such as cups of camel milk drunk, or minutes of reading.
Write down the seven numbers you collect.
Draw a labelled number line and place one dot above each value, stacking repeats.
Read your dot plot: what value happened most often, and what is the range?

activities.title: Tally vs Dot Plot Game (Family or Group)
activities.materials: number cards or a small pile of counting objects, and two players.
steps: Collect a set of numbers, for example how many siblings each person in the room has.
Player 1 records the data as a tally-and-frequency chart. Player 2 records the same data as a dot plot.
Compare the two displays. Which was faster to make? Which shows the spread more clearly?
Talk about when you would choose each method in real life.

activities.title: Which Book Is Easier to Read? (Investigation)
activities.materials: two storybooks and a page of paper.
steps: Choose one page from each book. Decide what data shows a book is easier to read (for example, the number of short words, or words with fewer than five letters).
Count and tally your chosen data on each page.
Display the data in a small table so the two books sit side by side.
Write two sentences: what is the same about the books, and what is different?

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand tally charts and dot plots best? What was the trickiest part of collecting data fairly? Did you ever miss someone out or count twice, and how did you fix it? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Statistical Question Rule
rules.text: A statistical question is answered by collecting data. It has different answers, not one fixed answer. How many pets do people have? is statistical. What is 5 + 7? is not.

rules.title: Tally Mark Rule
rules.text: Draw straight lines and group them in fives. The fifth mark goes across the other four. So a five-group plus two extra lines means 7.

rules.title: Dot Plot Rule
rules.text: Draw and label a number line. Place one dot above a value for each time it appears. Stack repeated dots and keep them evenly spaced.

terms: Information collected to answer a question
A question answered by collecting data, with varied answers
A counting line, grouped in fives
How many times a value appears
A table of categories and their counts
Dots above a number line showing data
How the data is spread out
From the lowest value to the highest value
commonMistakes: Ask: do we expect different answers?
Tally marks in a long row
Group in fives, fifth mark across
Line dots up on squared paper
Always label and title the display
Tally for categories, dot plot for numbers

realProblems.prompt: Sofia surveys 25 families for the fair and records favourite cake flavour: mango 9, banana 7, chocolate 5, vanilla 4. Which flavour is most popular, and did she survey all 25 families? Show your working.
realProblems.answer: 1) Mango is most popular (9); total surveyed = 9 + 7 + 5 + 4 = 25, so yes, all 25 families.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Mango is most popular (9); total surveyed = 9 + 7 + 5 + 4 = 25, so yes, all 25 families.

realProblems.prompt: Omar tallies animals at a well near Kismaayo: camels IIII, goats IIII IIII II, cattle IIII I. Write each frequency and find how many animals there are altogether.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Aisha counts sweets given to 12 children: 3, 4, 3, 5, 4, 3, 4, 6, 3, 4, 5, 4. Make a frequency table and find the most common number of sweets.
realProblems.answer: Frequencies: 3 appears 4, 4 appears 5, 5 appears 2, 6 appears 1; most common is 4 sweets.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Frequencies: 3 appears 4, 4 appears 5, 5 appears 2, 6 appears 1; most common is 4 sweets.

realProblems.prompt: Two classes read books in a month. Class A: (0×2)+(1×3)+(2×4)+(3×3)+(4×2)+(5×1). Class B: (0×1)+(1×2)+(2×3)+(3×5)+(4×3)+(5×1). Work out each total and say which class read more.
realProblems.answer: Class A = 0 + 3 + 8 + 9 + 8 + 5 = 33; Class B = 0 + 2 + 6 + 15 + 12 + 5 = 40; Class B read more.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Class A = 0 + 3 + 8 + 9 + 8 + 5 = 33; Class B = 0 + 2 + 6 + 15 + 12 + 5 = 40; Class B read more.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan records minutes to walk to the masjid for 8 friends: 5, 10, 5, 15, 10, 5, 20, 10. Draw a dot plot, then give the most common time and the range.
realProblems.answer: Most common time is 5 minutes and 10 minutes (both 3 friends); range is from 5 to 20 minutes.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Most common time is 5 minutes and 10 minutes (both 3 friends); range is from 5 to 20 minutes.

realProblems.prompt: For that same score plot, how many children scored more than 8?
realProblems.answer: Use the section 2: core practice guidance and explain each step.
realProblems.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Use the section 2: core practice guidance and explain each step.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: These are shoe sizes in a small group: 34, 30, 32, 34, 34, 31, 33, 36, 34, 32. Build a tally-and-frequency chart, then name the most common size.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Frequencies: 30 appears 1, 31 appears 1, 32 appears 2, 33 appears 1, 34 appears 4, 36 appears 1; most common size is 34.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Data of siblings: 2, 1, 0, 3, 2, 1, 4, 2, 1, 0, 2, 5. Make a frequency table, find the most common number, and state the range.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Frequencies: 0 appears 2, 1 appears 3, 2 appears 4, 3 appears 1, 4 appears 1, 5 appears 1; most common is 2 siblings; range is from 0 to 5.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, using the words categories and numbers, when a tally chart is a better choice than a dot plot, and give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A tally chart is better for categories (words) such as favourite sport, because it sorts groups and gives exact counts; a dot plot is better for numbers in a small range such as number of pets, because it shows the spread.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A dot plot shows hours of sport: 0 has 1 dot, 1 has 2 dots, 2 has 3 dots, 3 has 3 dots, 4 has 2 dots, 6 has 1 dot. How many learners do more than 2 hours of sport? Show which towers you added.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: More than 2 hours means towers above 3, 4 and 6: 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 learners.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Dot Plots.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A dot plot is another clear way to display data, and it is especially good for numbers. In a dot plot, we draw a horizontal number line, and above each number we place one dot for every time that number appears in the data. If a value appears many times, the dots stack up on top of one another, making a little tower. The taller the tower, the more often that value happens. Here is how to create a dot plot, step by step:

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Reading Information from a Dot Plot.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Making a dot plot is only half the skill. The other half is reading one that somebody else has drawn. To read a dot plot, first look at the label on the number line so you understand what the numbers mean. Then count the dots above each number to find how many times that value appears. Then look for patterns, such as which value has the tallest tower. Finally, compare different values to see how the data is spread. Here is a dot plot showing how many hours of sport each learner does each week. Counting the towers…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, invent fresh questions, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why do these skills matter so much? Because data helps us understand the world and make fair, sensible choices. Scientists collect data when they do experiments. Nurses at a clinic in Mogadishu record how many children come for check-ups. Business people collect data about what customers like so they do not waste money making things nobody wants. When Sofia wants to bake cakes for the school fair, she does not guess which flavour to make. She *collects data* by asking people what they like, and then she decides. Good data leads to good decisions.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, a ruler, and some squared paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading. You must do it. Draw the tally marks, build the tables, plot the dots, and say your conclusions out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure. It is simply a signal that shows you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin our data adventure!
I can tell the difference between a statistical question and a question with only one answer.
I can plan an investigation: choose a question, decide what data to collect, and plan how to record it.
I can collect and record data systematically so it is accurate and easy to read.
I can create and read a tally chart, using tally marks correctly in groups of five.
I can turn tally marks into a frequency table and use it to answer questions.
